response:
★★★★½ (4.5/5 Stars)
# Review: Hachi & Biscuit, Asheville’s Cozy Hidden Fusion Gem

I drove 130 miles round-trip from Charlotte to check out Hachi & Biscuit, the tiny cottage restaurant that’s been blowing up Appalachian food social media, and I’d make the drive again without a second thought. My sister and I booked a reservation six weeks in advance for her 30th birthday, and walking through its pastel blue front door felt like stepping into our favorite aunt’s sunlit, well-loved kitchen.

The 25-seat spot is crammed into a renovated shotgun cottage, with hand-thrown ceramic plates lining exposed brick walls, a crackling wood stove wafting cedar scent through the main dining room, and soft Stan Getz playing low over the speakers. Each table has a mason jar of foraged wild clover and daisies, and the only neon sign blinks softly: “Biscuits > Everything.” There’s no stuffy fine dining formality here—servers wear frayed vintage denim aprons, and the owner, Chef Maki Tate, popped out twice to check on tables, introducing herself to our group with a warm, flour-dusted grin.

Our server, Lila, was a little fluttery right off the bat: she dropped a water glass within five minutes of sitting down, and forgot our initial cocktail order twice. But she owned every misstep immediately, comped our round of house ginger beer (spiked with local bourbon, no less) to make amends, and knew every single detail on the chalkboard menu by heart. She walked us through the restaurant’s origin story: Chef Tate grew up in Atlanta, the daughter of Japanese immigrant parents, and spent years working in both kaiseki kitchens and Southern barbecue joints, dreaming up a spot where she could blend her two heritages without forced, gimmicky fusion.

We started with complimentary warm buttermilk biscuits, slathered with wildflower honey butter infused with a hint of matcha. They were flaky, layered, and still warm enough to melt the butter before we even ordered appetizers—no small feat after a two-hour drive. We split the Uni & Short Rib Biscuit Sliders: two mini buttermilk biscuit buns stuffed with braised short rib that’d been simmered in kombu,
